next_steps: Interventional radiology consultation for diagnostic portal venography with pressure measurements
Selective mesenteric angiography to evaluate for active contrast extravasation or vascular anomalies
Balloon-assisted enteroscopy to visualize the biliary-enteric anastomosis site and distal small bowel
Repeat cross-sectional imaging (CT or MRI) focusing on the venous phase to confirm portomesenteric hemodynamics
Consideration of liver biopsy if suspicion for underlying occult biliary or hepatic parenchymal disease persists

differential_diagnoses: Hemobilia secondary to portal biliopathy (biliary varices eroding into the hepaticojejunostomy)
Ectopic variceal hemorrhage (jejunal varices at the hepaticojejunostomy site)
Pseudoaneurysm or vascular-biliary fistula associated with prior hepaticojejunostomy
Segmental portal hypertension causing localized intestinal congestion/ulceration
Occult gastrointestinal neoplasm (e.g., small bowel GIST) missed on capsule endoscopy
next_steps: Interventional radiology consultation for diagnostic portal venography and potential transhepatic access to the biliary system
Selective angiography (celiac/mesenteric) during an active bleeding episode if possible
Double-balloon or single-balloon enteroscopy to specifically inspect the hepaticojejunostomy anastomosis site and afferent/efferent limbs
Review of historical surgical operative report from the hepaticojejunostomy procedure to assess for vascular proximity to the anastomosis
Testing for acquired von Willebrand syndrome (vWF antigen, activity, and multimers) in the setting of portal hypertension
Multidisciplinary review involving hepatobiliary surgery and interventional radiology to assess for surgical revision of the hepaticojejunostomy
